1	# Implement Engine Cost Analysis — April 25, 2026
2	
3	Investigation into why implement engine session costs rose ~12× from early April to late April.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Per-Day Cost and Token Breakdown (completed implement rigs)
8	
9	| Day | n | avg $ | output ktok | cache_read ktok | cache_write ktok |
10	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
11	| Apr 4  |  8 | $0.65 |   8.4k |   668k |  32k |
12	| Apr 6  | 14 | $1.24 |  18.7k |  1813k |  54k |
13	| Apr 7  | 19 | $3.25 |  41.6k |  3651k |  97k |
14	| Apr 8  |  7 | $3.24 |  40.5k |  5408k |  97k |
15	| Apr 9  | 12 | $2.21 |  21.0k |  2219k |  67k |
16	| Apr 10 | 20 | $1.79 |  15.5k |  1770k |  53k |
17	| Apr 13 |  2 | $2.14 |  14.4k |  2235k |  88k |
18	| Apr 14 |  3 | $3.06 |  19.9k |  4005k |  71k |
19	| Apr 15 |  5 | $4.17 |  23.1k |  4716k |  88k |
20	| **Apr 16** | **10** | **$4.80** | **33.6k** | **5636k** | **140k** | ← breakpoint |
21	| **Apr 17** |  **7** | **$8.08** | **56.8k** | **9879k** | **239k** | ← step-change |
22	| Apr 18 |  2 | $10.38 |  72.1k | 13008k | 269k |
23	| Apr 19 |  3 |  $4.98 |  31.8k |  6446k | 140k |
24	| Apr 21 |  9 |  $6.93 |  47.0k |  8440k | 219k |
25	| Apr 22 |  1 | $24.27 | 115.2k | 38163k | 369k |
26	| Apr 23 | 21 |  $5.62 |  33.1k |  7808k | 142k |
27	| Apr 24 | 30 |  $7.99 |  35.1k | 12201k | 147k |
28	| Apr 25 |  4 |  $8.56 |  50.4k | 11934k | 212k |
29	
30	**Summary:** avg cost $0.65 → $8.56 (13×). cache_read tokens 668k → 11,934k (18×). Output tokens 8k → 50k (6×).
31	
32	---
33	
34	## Breakpoint Identification
35	
36	**Apr 16–17** is the clear step-change: avg cost doubles in one day ($4.80 → $8.08), output tokens 1.7×, cache reads 1.75×. All three token categories move together, consistent with longer sessions rather than a system prompt size change.
37	
38	---
39	
40	## Hypotheses Explored
41	
42	### ❌ System prompt size explosion
43	The Loom assembles: charter + tool instructions + role doc. In vibers:
44	- No `charter.md` exists → zero charter contribution
45	- `roles/artificer.md` = 1,522 bytes
46	- The `artificer` role has `clerk:*` and `tools:*` permissions — modest tool set
47	
48	System prompt is small and didn't change around Apr 16-17. **Not the cause.**
49	
50	### ❌ givensSpec input size growth
51	Checked givensSpec total sizes for implement sessions across the timeline:
52	- Apr 6:  41,447 chars
53	- Apr 10: 21,152 chars
54	- Apr 17: 20,507 chars / 26,489 chars
55	- Apr 23:  5,912 chars
56	
57	No correlation with cost. **Not the cause.**
58	
59	### ❌ Repo size as primary driver
60	Repo grew 4.3× (1.0MB → 4.3MB, 144 → 335 files) over the period. Cost grew 13×. Repo growth is a headwind but doesn't explain the discrete Apr 16-17 step-change. **Partial factor, not primary cause.**
61	
62	### ✅ Session length explosion → cache read amplification
63	The primary mechanism: longer sessions cause Claude's automatic cache checkpoints to be re-read on every subsequent turn. Cache reads are cheap per-token ($0.30/Mtok) but the volume grew 18× — from 668k to 12M tokens avg per session. This is a **session length effect**: each additional turn re-reads all previous cache checkpoints.
64	
65	---
66	
67	## Root Cause: Two Commits on Apr 16–17
68	
69	### Commit 1: `920e65ca` — Apr 16 04:43
70	**"feat(astrolabe,spider): add GSD-style task manifest to planner output"**
71	
72	The sage-writer now appends a `<task-manifest>` XML block (3–8 tasks) to every planning output. Each task has `<verify>` (an executable command) and `<done>` (outcome criterion).
73	
74	The implement engine's `EXECUTION_EPILOGUE` changed from:
75	```
76	\n\nCommit all changes before ending your session.
77	```
78	to structured rules: work task-by-task, run each `<verify>` command after each task, commit after each task.
79	
80	**Behavioural shift:**
81	- Before: read writ → write code → commit → done
82	- After: for each task: write code → run verify → review output → commit → repeat
83	
84	This turned a bounded single-pass session into an iterative multi-task loop.
85	
86	### Commit 2: `260f5cf9` — Apr 17 17:01
87	**"sage-writer: inline click content, do not preserve ids in the brief"**
88	
89	Paired with `da0e460` granting sage roles `ratchet:read`. The sage-writer now resolves every click reference in the brief/decisions (`click-extract`, `click-show`) and inlines the full rationale into the spec, making the planning output and the `writ.body` substantially larger and more detailed.
90	
91	This fed forward into implement sessions (especially via `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}` in the plan-and-ship rig, introduced Apr 18-19), producing longer and richer prompts for the implementer.
92	
93	---
94	
95	## Quality vs Cost Tradeoff
96	
97	The cost increase coincided with **measurably better work**:
98	
99	| Metric | pre-Apr16 | post-Apr16 |
100	|---|---|---|
101	| Rig success rate | 83% | 94% |
102	| Avg git commits/session | 0.8 | 1.7 |
103	| Multi-commit sessions | 4% | ~20% |
104	| Avg turns/session | 77 | ~150 |
105	| Avg verify calls/session | 5.5 | 16 |
106	| Zero-commit sessions | ~20% | ~5% |
107	
108	The success rate improvement (83% → 94%) and the near-elimination of zero-commit sessions suggest the structured task manifest is producing better implementation discipline. The cost is real but not waste.
109	
110	**Verify call distribution:** Average position of verify calls in session timeline was 0.58 pre and 0.58 post — identical. The task manifest didn't move verification to earlier in sessions; it **amplified** behavior already latent. What changed was volume (5.5 → 16 verify calls avg), not placement. The agents were already verifying incrementally.
111	
112	---
113	
114	## Recommendations to Reduce Cost (ranked by impact vs effort)
115	
116	### 1. Verify engine as a separate rig stage ★★★ (high impact, clean architecture)
117	Split the rig: `implement` (write code, commit — no tests) + `verify` engine (fresh session, runs test suite, reports pass/fail). If verify fails, post a narrow patch mandate. Implement sessions get dramatically cheaper — bounded by "write and commit." Verification still happens but in a context-free session where it's cheap. This is architecturally correct: what-to-build vs did-it-work are separate concerns. The `implement-loop` / `piece-session` plumbing from Apr 16 (`8d2f548`) was exploring this direction before it was disabled (`fb64473`).
118	
119	### 2. Narrow the verify commands in the task manifest ★★★ (medium impact, low effort)
120	The manifest currently doesn't constrain scope — `pnpm -w test` runs the entire monorepo suite every task. Requiring the sage-writer to produce `pnpm --filter @shardworks/affected-package test` (package-scoped) instead would cut tool_result size, execution time, and the output token cost of reading test results. Change is in `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.
121	
122	### 3. Checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture ★★ (high impact, medium complexity)
123	After each task commit, end the session and start a fresh one with a compact handoff: "tasks t1-t3 complete, here's the git log, continue from t4." Each session's cache starts small and doesn't compound. This is implement-loop done right — cost per piece is bounded. With 5-task manifests, this could be 5 × $1 instead of 1 × $8. Complexity: the handoff prompt needs enough context for orientation without re-reading everything.
124	
125	### 4. Defer all verification to seal engine ★★ (medium impact, easy, quality tradeoff)
126	The `seal` engine runs next before merging. Add a test-run as seal's first step. If tests fail, seal fails and posts a narrowly-scoped patch mandate. Implement becomes: write code → commit → done. Cheaper per session. Tradeoff: seal doesn't have the implementation context for intelligent failure diagnosis. Mid-task test failures caught late are harder to fix. Acceptable if seal failures are rare.
127	
128	### 5. Verify-reasoning subagent for failures only ★ (targeted, low effort)
129	Normal `pnpm test` runs inline (cheap). When a verify step *fails*, spawn a subagent: "here's the test failure, here's the diff, diagnose and commit a fix." The diagnostic chain-of-thought stays in the subagent; only the fix summary returns to the parent. Subagents don't help for pure command execution (output lands in parent context anyway) but help when failure-diagnosis reasoning is substantial.
130	
131	### 6. Bound manifest body size ★ (small improvement, very low effort)
132	Require sage-writer to cap total manifest to 6 tasks and `<action>` fields to 150 words each. Smaller initial prompt → smaller turn-1 cache write → cheaper every-turn cache re-read. Add to `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.
133	
134	---
135	
136	## Current Understanding
137	
138	The implement engine is paying for **deliberateness**. The task manifest made sessions longer, more incremental, and better. The cost mechanism is Claude's cache being re-read on every additional turn — not a system prompt explosion, not input size growth, but compound re-reading of a growing conversation history. Every verify cycle (2-3 turns) adds to the stack that every subsequent turn has to re-read.
139	
140	The best intervention is structural: separate implementation (expensive, context-rich) from verification (cheap, context-free) as distinct rig stages. This preserves the quality gains while cutting implement session length roughly in half.
141

# Implement Engine Cost Analysis — April 25, 2026

Investigation into why implement engine session costs rose ~12× from early April to late April.

---

## Per-Day Cost and Token Breakdown (completed implement rigs)

| Day | n | avg $ | output ktok | cache_read ktok | cache_write ktok |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Apr 4  |  8 | $0.65 |   8.4k |   668k |  32k |
| Apr 6  | 14 | $1.24 |  18.7k |  1813k |  54k |
| Apr 7  | 19 | $3.25 |  41.6k |  3651k |  97k |
| Apr 8  |  7 | $3.24 |  40.5k |  5408k |  97k |
| Apr 9  | 12 | $2.21 |  21.0k |  2219k |  67k |
| Apr 10 | 20 | $1.79 |  15.5k |  1770k |  53k |
| Apr 13 |  2 | $2.14 |  14.4k |  2235k |  88k |
| Apr 14 |  3 | $3.06 |  19.9k |  4005k |  71k |
| Apr 15 |  5 | $4.17 |  23.1k |  4716k |  88k |
| **Apr 16** | **10** | **$4.80** | **33.6k** | **5636k** | **140k** | ← breakpoint |
| **Apr 17** |  **7** | **$8.08** | **56.8k** | **9879k** | **239k** | ← step-change |
| Apr 18 |  2 | $10.38 |  72.1k | 13008k | 269k |
| Apr 19 |  3 |  $4.98 |  31.8k |  6446k | 140k |
| Apr 21 |  9 |  $6.93 |  47.0k |  8440k | 219k |
| Apr 22 |  1 | $24.27 | 115.2k | 38163k | 369k |
| Apr 23 | 21 |  $5.62 |  33.1k |  7808k | 142k |
| Apr 24 | 30 |  $7.99 |  35.1k | 12201k | 147k |
| Apr 25 |  4 |  $8.56 |  50.4k | 11934k | 212k |

**Summary:** avg cost $0.65 → $8.56 (13×). cache_read tokens 668k → 11,934k (18×). Output tokens 8k → 50k (6×).

---

## Breakpoint Identification

**Apr 16–17** is the clear step-change: avg cost doubles in one day ($4.80 → $8.08), output tokens 1.7×, cache reads 1.75×. All three token categories move together, consistent with longer sessions rather than a system prompt size change.

---

## Hypotheses Explored

### ❌ System prompt size explosion
The Loom assembles: charter + tool instructions + role doc. In vibers:
- No `charter.md` exists → zero charter contribution
- `roles/artificer.md` = 1,522 bytes
- The `artificer` role has `clerk:*` and `tools:*` permissions — modest tool set

System prompt is small and didn't change around Apr 16-17. **Not the cause.**

### ❌ givensSpec input size growth
Checked givensSpec total sizes for implement sessions across the timeline:
- Apr 6:  41,447 chars
- Apr 10: 21,152 chars
- Apr 17: 20,507 chars / 26,489 chars
- Apr 23:  5,912 chars

No correlation with cost. **Not the cause.**

### ❌ Repo size as primary driver
Repo grew 4.3× (1.0MB → 4.3MB, 144 → 335 files) over the period. Cost grew 13×. Repo growth is a headwind but doesn't explain the discrete Apr 16-17 step-change. **Partial factor, not primary cause.**

### ✅ Session length explosion → cache read amplification
The primary mechanism: longer sessions cause Claude's automatic cache checkpoints to be re-read on every subsequent turn. Cache reads are cheap per-token ($0.30/Mtok) but the volume grew 18× — from 668k to 12M tokens avg per session. This is a **session length effect**: each additional turn re-reads all previous cache checkpoints.

---

## Root Cause: Two Commits on Apr 16–17

### Commit 1: `920e65ca` — Apr 16 04:43
**"feat(astrolabe,spider): add GSD-style task manifest to planner output"**

The sage-writer now appends a `<task-manifest>` XML block (3–8 tasks) to every planning output. Each task has `<verify>` (an executable command) and `<done>` (outcome criterion).

The implement engine's `EXECUTION_EPILOGUE` changed from:
```
\n\nCommit all changes before ending your session.
```
to structured rules: work task-by-task, run each `<verify>` command after each task, commit after each task.

**Behavioural shift:**
- Before: read writ → write code → commit → done
- After: for each task: write code → run verify → review output → commit → repeat

This turned a bounded single-pass session into an iterative multi-task loop.

### Commit 2: `260f5cf9` — Apr 17 17:01
**"sage-writer: inline click content, do not preserve ids in the brief"**

Paired with `da0e460` granting sage roles `ratchet:read`. The sage-writer now resolves every click reference in the brief/decisions (`click-extract`, `click-show`) and inlines the full rationale into the spec, making the planning output and the `writ.body` substantially larger and more detailed.

This fed forward into implement sessions (especially via `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}` in the plan-and-ship rig, introduced Apr 18-19), producing longer and richer prompts for the implementer.

---

## Quality vs Cost Tradeoff

The cost increase coincided with **measurably better work**:

| Metric | pre-Apr16 | post-Apr16 |
|---|---|---|
| Rig success rate | 83% | 94% |
| Avg git commits/session | 0.8 | 1.7 |
| Multi-commit sessions | 4% | ~20% |
| Avg turns/session | 77 | ~150 |
| Avg verify calls/session | 5.5 | 16 |
| Zero-commit sessions | ~20% | ~5% |

The success rate improvement (83% → 94%) and the near-elimination of zero-commit sessions suggest the structured task manifest is producing better implementation discipline. The cost is real but not waste.

**Verify call distribution:** Average position of verify calls in session timeline was 0.58 pre and 0.58 post — identical. The task manifest didn't move verification to earlier in sessions; it **amplified** behavior already latent. What changed was volume (5.5 → 16 verify calls avg), not placement. The agents were already verifying incrementally.

---

## Recommendations to Reduce Cost (ranked by impact vs effort)

### 1. Verify engine as a separate rig stage ★★★ (high impact, clean architecture)
Split the rig: `implement` (write code, commit — no tests) + `verify` engine (fresh session, runs test suite, reports pass/fail). If verify fails, post a narrow patch mandate. Implement sessions get dramatically cheaper — bounded by "write and commit." Verification still happens but in a context-free session where it's cheap. This is architecturally correct: what-to-build vs did-it-work are separate concerns. The `implement-loop` / `piece-session` plumbing from Apr 16 (`8d2f548`) was exploring this direction before it was disabled (`fb64473`).

### 2. Narrow the verify commands in the task manifest ★★★ (medium impact, low effort)
The manifest currently doesn't constrain scope — `pnpm -w test` runs the entire monorepo suite every task. Requiring the sage-writer to produce `pnpm --filter @shardworks/affected-package test` (package-scoped) instead would cut tool_result size, execution time, and the output token cost of reading test results. Change is in `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.

### 3. Checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture ★★ (high impact, medium complexity)
After each task commit, end the session and start a fresh one with a compact handoff: "tasks t1-t3 complete, here's the git log, continue from t4." Each session's cache starts small and doesn't compound. This is implement-loop done right — cost per piece is bounded. With 5-task manifests, this could be 5 × $1 instead of 1 × $8. Complexity: the handoff prompt needs enough context for orientation without re-reading everything.

### 4. Defer all verification to seal engine ★★ (medium impact, easy, quality tradeoff)
The `seal` engine runs next before merging. Add a test-run as seal's first step. If tests fail, seal fails and posts a narrowly-scoped patch mandate. Implement becomes: write code → commit → done. Cheaper per session. Tradeoff: seal doesn't have the implementation context for intelligent failure diagnosis. Mid-task test failures caught late are harder to fix. Acceptable if seal failures are rare.

### 5. Verify-reasoning subagent for failures only ★ (targeted, low effort)
Normal `pnpm test` runs inline (cheap). When a verify step *fails*, spawn a subagent: "here's the test failure, here's the diff, diagnose and commit a fix." The diagnostic chain-of-thought stays in the subagent; only the fix summary returns to the parent. Subagents don't help for pure command execution (output lands in parent context anyway) but help when failure-diagnosis reasoning is substantial.

### 6. Bound manifest body size ★ (small improvement, very low effort)
Require sage-writer to cap total manifest to 6 tasks and `<action>` fields to 150 words each. Smaller initial prompt → smaller turn-1 cache write → cheaper every-turn cache re-read. Add to `sage-writer.md` manifest rules.

---

## Current Understanding

The implement engine is paying for **deliberateness**. The task manifest made sessions longer, more incremental, and better. The cost mechanism is Claude's cache being re-read on every additional turn — not a system prompt explosion, not input size growth, but compound re-reading of a growing conversation history. Every verify cycle (2-3 turns) adds to the stack that every subsequent turn has to re-read.

The best intervention is structural: separate implementation (expensive, context-rich) from verification (cheap, context-free) as distinct rig stages. This preserves the quality gains while cutting implement session length roughly in half.

content.text:
Read the doc. Here's my call:

## Recommendation: **Option 3 — Checkpoint-and-fresh-session (piece-sessions)**

### Why this one

The diagnosis is the load-bearing claim: cost grew because *cache reads compound with conversation length*. Every verify cycle adds 2-3 turns, and every subsequent turn re-reads everything that came before. That's the mechanism.

Of the six options, only **#3 attacks the mechanism directly**. The others either work around it (defer verification, narrow output) or paper over it (subagents for failures). #3 says: don't let the conversation grow long in the first place. Each task gets its own session with a fresh, small cache. The compounding stops being a thing.

### Why not the others

- **#1 (separate verify rig)** is architecturally clean but has a hidden cost: it removes per-task verification from the implement loop. The quality data is unambiguous — verify calls went 5.5 → 16/session and zero-commit sessions dropped 20% → 5%. That's the *deliberateness* tax paying off. Pulling verify out into a separate rig means failures get caught after-the-fact, in a context-free session that has to reverse-engineer "what was the implementer trying to do at task 3?" You'd lose the quality gain that the cost is buying.

- **#2 (narrow verify commands)** is a fine quick win and I'd ship it regardless, but it's a multiplier on a constant — it shaves output tokens, not the cache-read curve. Cost still grows with session length, just from a slightly lower baseline.

- **#4 (defer to seal)** has the same problem as #1, worse — failure detection moves even further from the implementation context.

- **#5 (subagent for failures)** is targeted but only helps when failure-diagnosis reasoning is substantial. Most verify failures are "test red, fix obvious thing." Doesn't move the needle.

- **#6 (bound manifest size)** is a rounding error.

### The case for #3 specifically

- **Preserves the quality gain.** Each piece-session still verifies in-context. The agent doing task 3 can see that task 3's tests pass before it commits.
- **Bounded cost per piece.** 5 tasks × ~$1 each is ~$5, vs 1 × $8 today. The cost becomes linear in tasks, not super-linear in turns.
- **You were already building toward this.** `implement-loop` / piece-session plumbing landed Apr 16 (`8d2f548`) and got disabled (`fb64473`). The infrastructure exists at some maturity level — this is finishing what was started, not starting fresh.
- **Handoff design is the real work** — the compact orientation prompt for piece N+1 ("tasks t1-t3 complete, here's the git log, here's the manifest, continue from t4"). That's where the actual difficulty lives, but it's a tractable design problem and one we'd want to solve anyway for any session-resume capability.

### What I'd actually do

1. **Ship #2 today** as a stopgap — it's a 30-line edit in `sage-writer.md` to scope verify commands to `--filter @<package>`. Cuts tool_result size now while #3 gets designed.
2. **Pull #3 off the shelf** — figure out what the implement-loop plumbing actually does in its current state and what's missing for the handoff prompt design.

### What I'm not sure about

I haven't read the framework-side implement-loop code (`/workspace/nexus/`), so I'm taking the doc's claim at face value that the plumbing is mostly there. If the existing code is dead-end and #3 is a from-scratch build, the calculus shifts — #2 + #1 (split rig, accept the quality tradeoff, monitor it) becomes more attractive because it's ship-this-week vs ship-next-month.

Worth checking: how much of `8d2f548` is salvageable, and why was it disabled in `fb64473`? If it was disabled for a reason that still applies, #3 has more debt than the doc implies.

commit fb64473e957ebea2bd2d595632c703bd300161e0
Author: Artificer <w-mo221rxe-15e5133df0cf@nexus.local>
Date:   Thu Apr 16 22:43:29 2026 +0000

    feat(astrolabe): disable piece-child creation and manifest stripping in spec-publish
    
    The spec-publish engine now posts the generated spec verbatim as a single
    mandate writ — regardless of whether the spec contains a <task-manifest>
    block. Previously, the presence of a manifest triggered a piece-aware path
    that stripped the manifest from the mandate body and fanned each <task>
    out into a child piece writ via a draft→open dance.
    
    With this change:
    - The mandate body always equals plan.spec (task-manifest preserved).
    - No child piece writs are auto-created by the planning pipeline.
    - The mandate is posted live (no draft state, no transition call).
    
    Pieces remain a first-class writ type and can still be created on demand
    (e.g. via the piece-add tool) by downstream artificers acting on the
    manifest themselves. The parseTaskManifest helper and its tests are
    removed since nothing consumes them anymore.

 packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/engines/index.ts    |   2 +-

commit fb64473e957ebea2bd2d595632c703bd300161e0
Author: Artificer <w-mo221rxe-15e5133df0cf@nexus.local>
Date:   Thu Apr 16 22:43:29 2026 +0000

    feat(astrolabe): disable piece-child creation and manifest stripping in spec-publish
    
    The spec-publish engine now posts the generated spec verbatim as a single
    mandate writ — regardless of whether the spec contains a <task-manifest>
    block. Previously, the presence of a manifest triggered a piece-aware path
    that stripped the manifest from the mandate body and fanned each <task>
    out into a child piece writ via a draft→open dance.
    
    With this change:
    - The mandate body always equals plan.spec (task-manifest preserved).
    - No child piece writs are auto-created by the planning pipeline.
    - The mandate is posted live (no draft state, no transition call).
    
    Pieces remain a first-class writ type and can still be created on demand
    (e.g. via the piece-add tool) by downstream artificers acting on the
    manifest themselves. The parseTaskManifest helper and its tests are
    removed since nothing consumes them anymore.

content.content:
1	# X010 H1 — Cheap-Mining of Two Decomposed Rigs
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-04-16
4	**Hypothesis tested:** H1 — Long sessions accumulate marginal cost that staging avoids.
5	**Method:** Mine per-turn token usage from existing piece-session transcripts of two
6	decomposed rigs; project what a single-session (monolithic) run of the same work
7	would have cost by simulating cache-read accumulation.
8	**Click:** `c-mo1w0d9a-16ddf9904d2c` (H1 cheap-mining, concluded by this artifact)
9	**Related clicks:** `c-mo1w0g2n-4b206fe845e5` (expensive baseline, parked; triggered as `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205`)
10	
11	## Dataset
12	
13	Two decomposed rigs using `piece-session` engine with `implement-loop` orchestrator:
14	
15	| Rig | Writ | Spec | Pieces | Total turns | Observed cost | Wall time |
16	|---|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
17	| 1 | `w-mo1n7t3l-daa33f72a08c` | "piece engine shows as cancelled" fix | 5 | 150 | $6.92 | ~20 min |
18	| 2 | `w-mo1o65ky-683de2230313` | "Oculus click tree view" (25-decision spec) | 6 | 267 | $20.11 | ~48 min |
19	
20	Transcripts sourced from:
21	- `/home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mo1n7twn-84d9ea8d/`
22	- `/home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mo1o66gw-d09ff833/`
23	
24	Mapped to Laboratory session YAML records by end-time.
25	
26	## Method
27	
28	1. **Per-turn extraction.** For each assistant message in each transcript, record
29	   `input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, and
30	   `output_tokens`. Dedupe by `message.id` since Claude Code's transcript
31	   format writes each assistant message multiple times (streaming echoes).
32	
33	2. **Pricing model.** Blended Opus 4 estimates (see *Caveats* for the 3× absolute
34	   offset from actual): input $15/M, cache_create $18.75/M, cache_read $1.50/M,
35	   output $75/M. Ratios between scenarios are preserved even if absolute
36	   dollars are inflated.
37	
38	3. **Monolithic projection.** Simulate the same per-turn work in one continuous
39	   session. Each simulated turn carries `cache_read = cumulative_cache_create_from_prior_turns
40	   + initial_context`. Per-turn `input`, `cache_create`, and `output` are held constant
41	   from the observed decomposed data. This models the known effect that a continuous
42	   session must replay its full prior context on every turn.
43	
44	Scripts: `scripts/h1_mining.py` and `scripts/h1_projection.py` in this artifact directory.
45	
46	## Per-Session Results
47	
48	Dedup'd turn counts, summed token usage, and derived marginal measures:
49	
50	```
51	label    wall_s  turns      input   cache_create   cache_read    output   crd/turn   crd_slope
52	R1-P0      245s    39          44         46,292    1,692,668    10,888    43,402      960
53	R1-P1      130s    18          23         37,358      697,327     7,664    38,740    2,038
54	R1-P2      177s    28          38         89,739    1,232,190    12,483    44,007    1,852
55	R1-P3      138s    23          28         49,663    1,069,068     7,847    46,481    2,259
56	R1-P4      534s    42          47         71,090    2,491,126    20,109    59,313    1,546
57	R2-P0      142s    23          38         85,117    1,052,103     8,956    45,744    2,097
58	R2-P1       91s    20          25         56,253      887,741     4,576    44,387    2,566
59	R2-P2      305s    53          63        160,735    4,053,695    18,969    76,485    1,590
60	R2-P3      906s    50          60        169,736    4,057,278    38,484    81,146    2,504
61	R2-P4      694s    80          90        180,552    7,424,067    37,027    92,801    1,248
62	R2-P5      579s    41          51        163,057    3,113,061    24,492    75,928    2,087
63	```
64	
65	- `crd/turn` — mean cache-read tokens per turn within the session.
66	- `crd_slope` — linear-regression slope of `cache_read` vs `turn_index` (= new
67	  cache-read tokens added per additional turn).
68	
69	**`crd_slope` is remarkably consistent across sessions at 1,000–2,500 tokens/turn.**
70	This is the marginal context-drag: each additional turn must carry ~1.5–2.5K
71	more tokens of context than the previous one. Slope is session-agnostic; it
72	reflects the rate at which new information (tool outputs, file contents,
73	agent thinking) enters the persistent context window per turn of work.
74	
75	## Bucket-by-Turn-Index
76	
77	Averaging across all 11 piece-sessions by position within the session:
78	
79	| Turn | Sessions observed | Mean cache_read |
80	|---:|---:|---:|
81	| 1 | 11 | 14,440 |
82	| 10 | 11 | 50,924 |
83	| 20 | 10 | 65,186 |
84	| 30 | 10 | ~80,000 |
85	| 50 | 5 | ~95,000 |
86	| 60 | 3 | ~110,000 |
87	| 75 | 2 | ~110,000 |
88	
89	Growth is roughly linear through turn ~50 then flattens — likely because only
90	the longest session (R2-P4 at 80 turns) reaches those turns, and that session
91	probably hit auto-compaction. The visible linear slope in this cross-session
92	average is **≈1,500 tokens/turn**, consistent with the per-session slopes.
93	
94	## Monolithic Projection
95	
96	Simulated cost of the same work done in one continuous session:
97	
98	```
99	RIG 1 (150 total turns):
100	  Observed decomposed (model dollars):  $20.72
101	  Simulated monolithic (model dollars): $46.81
102	  Ratio:                                2.26×
103	  Final projected context:              308,582 tokens
104	
105	RIG 2 (267 total turns):
106	  Observed decomposed (model dollars):  $56.11
107	  Simulated monolithic (model dollars): $206.06
108	  Ratio:                                3.67×
109	  Final projected context:              829,890 tokens   ← 4× Opus 4 window
110	```
111	
112	Normalizing to real-money equivalents (apply the ~3× deflation factor observed
113	between the model and actual session YAML costs):
114	
115	| Rig | Observed (actual) | Projected monolithic (actual) | Ratio |
116	|---|---:|---:|---:|
117	| Rig 1 | $6.92 | ~$15.60 | 2.3× |
118	| Rig 2 | $20.11 | ~$69 | 3.4× |
119	
120	The ratio grows with total work because cache-read drag compounds quadratically
121	in a single session (every prior turn is re-read on every subsequent turn).
122	
123	## Findings
124	
125	**H1 (weak form) is confirmed.** Cache-read grows linearly within sessions at
126	~1,500–2,500 tokens/turn. This produces a real but gentle per-turn cost
127	increase — ~$0.002 extra per turn at current cache-read pricing, compounding
128	across hundreds of turns into a 2–4× aggregate overhead for monolithic
129	execution on the same total work.
130	
131	**H1 (strong form — catastrophic failure above a context threshold) is
132	implicated but not proven by mining alone.** The Rig 2 monolithic projection
133	of 830K tokens of context exceeds Claude Opus 4's ~200K window by 4×. A real
134	monolithic run of the Oculus spec would have to hit one of three failure
135	modes around turn 75–100:
136	
137	1. Hard context-window error (engine fails, session ends).
138	2. Auto-compaction with loss of earlier context (quality cost).
139	3. Self-imposed checkpoint/handoff patterns by the agent (staging-in-prompt).
140	
141	The currently-running `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205` — a single-session rerun of the
142	same Oculus spec using the older `implement` engine — will provide the
143	empirical data point for which failure mode dominates.
144	
145	## Caveats
146	
147	1. **Pricing model is off by ~3× from actual.** My estimates inflate absolute
148	   dollar values by a factor of ~3 relative to what Claude billed (per the
149	   session YAML `cost_usd` fields). Possible causes: blended tier assumptions,
150	   Claude Code applying additional caching discounts I haven't modeled, or
151	   model-class differences. Ratios between scenarios are preserved.
152	
153	2. **The monolithic simulation is idealized.** It assumes each turn's per-turn
154	   `input` and `output` work would be identical in a single session — in
155	   reality, a monolithic agent might take fewer turns (less orientation
156	   overhead) or more turns (confused by accumulated context). The projection
157	   is best read as a "what if the work pattern held constant" estimate, not
158	   a prediction.
159	
160	3. **Claude's cache retrieval isn't perfect.** Real monolithic runs may show
161	   slightly lower cache_read than the simulation projects because of
162	   pruning, TTL expiry, or optimization. The simulation is a reasonable
163	   upper bound on context drag.
164	
165	4. **No quality comparison.** This analysis is pure cost-and-volume. H2 (whether
166	   decomposed runs produce *better* output than monolithic runs) is unaddressed
167	   here and remains a separate parked inquiry.
168	
169	5. **Two rigs is a small sample.** The `crd_slope` consistency across 11
170	   sessions gives some confidence it generalizes, but a richer corpus (the
171	   2026-04-03 analysis has 71 sessions) should be cross-checked. Notably the
172	   earlier artifact concluded "economic case for staged sessions is weak at
173	   current session lengths" — this newer analysis is more bullish on staging,
174	   and the difference is worth reconciling. Likely reasons: the 2026-04-03
175	   analysis didn't project monolithic costs for very long sessions (the
176	   dataset cut off well below the 267-turn Rig 2 scale), and didn't account
177	   for the hard context-window ceiling that dominates at that scale.
178	
179	## Next Steps
180	
181	- **Conclude `c-mo1w0d9a-16ddf9904d2c`** (this click) with the H1-weak-confirmed
182	  finding.
183	- **Wait for `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205`** to complete — its outcome is the
184	  H1-strong data point.
185	- **Reconcile with 2026-04-03 analysis** at some point, since the two arrive
186	  at different conclusions.
187	- **Design staged-sessions threshold** (parked H4 click `c-mo1w0rfk-2e0eeb3a1460`)
188	  — the crd_slope gives a first-order estimate: staging pays off above roughly
189	  `(200K context limit) / (crd_slope ≈ 2K per turn) ≈ 100 turns per session`.
190	  Rig 2's pieces averaged 44 turns — well below. A monolithic Rig 2 at 267
191	  turns is well above.
192

# X010 H1 — Cheap-Mining of Two Decomposed Rigs

**Date:** 2026-04-16
**Hypothesis tested:** H1 — Long sessions accumulate marginal cost that staging avoids.
**Method:** Mine per-turn token usage from existing piece-session transcripts of two
decomposed rigs; project what a single-session (monolithic) run of the same work
would have cost by simulating cache-read accumulation.
**Click:** `c-mo1w0d9a-16ddf9904d2c` (H1 cheap-mining, concluded by this artifact)
**Related clicks:** `c-mo1w0g2n-4b206fe845e5` (expensive baseline, parked; triggered as `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205`)

## Dataset

Two decomposed rigs using `piece-session` engine with `implement-loop` orchestrator:

| Rig | Writ | Spec | Pieces | Total turns | Observed cost | Wall time |
|---|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | `w-mo1n7t3l-daa33f72a08c` | "piece engine shows as cancelled" fix | 5 | 150 | $6.92 | ~20 min |
| 2 | `w-mo1o65ky-683de2230313` | "Oculus click tree view" (25-decision spec) | 6 | 267 | $20.11 | ~48 min |

Transcripts sourced from:
- `/home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mo1n7twn-84d9ea8d/`
- `/home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mo1o66gw-d09ff833/`

Mapped to Laboratory session YAML records by end-time.

## Method

1. **Per-turn extraction.** For each assistant message in each transcript, record
   `input_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, and
   `output_tokens`. Dedupe by `message.id` since Claude Code's transcript
   format writes each assistant message multiple times (streaming echoes).

2. **Pricing model.** Blended Opus 4 estimates (see *Caveats* for the 3× absolute
   offset from actual): input $15/M, cache_create $18.75/M, cache_read $1.50/M,
   output $75/M. Ratios between scenarios are preserved even if absolute
   dollars are inflated.

3. **Monolithic projection.** Simulate the same per-turn work in one continuous
   session. Each simulated turn carries `cache_read = cumulative_cache_create_from_prior_turns
   + initial_context`. Per-turn `input`, `cache_create`, and `output` are held constant
   from the observed decomposed data. This models the known effect that a continuous
   session must replay its full prior context on every turn.

Scripts: `scripts/h1_mining.py` and `scripts/h1_projection.py` in this artifact directory.

## Per-Session Results

Dedup'd turn counts, summed token usage, and derived marginal measures:

```
label    wall_s  turns      input   cache_create   cache_read    output   crd/turn   crd_slope
R1-P0      245s    39          44         46,292    1,692,668    10,888    43,402      960
R1-P1      130s    18          23         37,358      697,327     7,664    38,740    2,038
R1-P2      177s    28          38         89,739    1,232,190    12,483    44,007    1,852
R1-P3      138s    23          28         49,663    1,069,068     7,847    46,481    2,259
R1-P4      534s    42          47         71,090    2,491,126    20,109    59,313    1,546
R2-P0      142s    23          38         85,117    1,052,103     8,956    45,744    2,097
R2-P1       91s    20          25         56,253      887,741     4,576    44,387    2,566
R2-P2      305s    53          63        160,735    4,053,695    18,969    76,485    1,590
R2-P3      906s    50          60        169,736    4,057,278    38,484    81,146    2,504
R2-P4      694s    80          90        180,552    7,424,067    37,027    92,801    1,248
R2-P5      579s    41          51        163,057    3,113,061    24,492    75,928    2,087
```

- `crd/turn` — mean cache-read tokens per turn within the session.
- `crd_slope` — linear-regression slope of `cache_read` vs `turn_index` (= new
  cache-read tokens added per additional turn).

**`crd_slope` is remarkably consistent across sessions at 1,000–2,500 tokens/turn.**
This is the marginal context-drag: each additional turn must carry ~1.5–2.5K
more tokens of context than the previous one. Slope is session-agnostic; it
reflects the rate at which new information (tool outputs, file contents,
agent thinking) enters the persistent context window per turn of work.

## Bucket-by-Turn-Index

Averaging across all 11 piece-sessions by position within the session:

| Turn | Sessions observed | Mean cache_read |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 1 | 11 | 14,440 |
| 10 | 11 | 50,924 |
| 20 | 10 | 65,186 |
| 30 | 10 | ~80,000 |
| 50 | 5 | ~95,000 |
| 60 | 3 | ~110,000 |
| 75 | 2 | ~110,000 |

Growth is roughly linear through turn ~50 then flattens — likely because only
the longest session (R2-P4 at 80 turns) reaches those turns, and that session
probably hit auto-compaction. The visible linear slope in this cross-session
average is **≈1,500 tokens/turn**, consistent with the per-session slopes.

## Monolithic Projection

Simulated cost of the same work done in one continuous session:

```
RIG 1 (150 total turns):
  Observed decomposed (model dollars):  $20.72
  Simulated monolithic (model dollars): $46.81
  Ratio:                                2.26×
  Final projected context:              308,582 tokens

RIG 2 (267 total turns):
  Observed decomposed (model dollars):  $56.11
  Simulated monolithic (model dollars): $206.06
  Ratio:                                3.67×
  Final projected context:              829,890 tokens   ← 4× Opus 4 window
```

Normalizing to real-money equivalents (apply the ~3× deflation factor observed
between the model and actual session YAML costs):

| Rig | Observed (actual) | Projected monolithic (actual) | Ratio |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Rig 1 | $6.92 | ~$15.60 | 2.3× |
| Rig 2 | $20.11 | ~$69 | 3.4× |

The ratio grows with total work because cache-read drag compounds quadratically
in a single session (every prior turn is re-read on every subsequent turn).

## Findings

**H1 (weak form) is confirmed.** Cache-read grows linearly within sessions at
~1,500–2,500 tokens/turn. This produces a real but gentle per-turn cost
increase — ~$0.002 extra per turn at current cache-read pricing, compounding
across hundreds of turns into a 2–4× aggregate overhead for monolithic
execution on the same total work.

**H1 (strong form — catastrophic failure above a context threshold) is
implicated but not proven by mining alone.** The Rig 2 monolithic projection
of 830K tokens of context exceeds Claude Opus 4's ~200K window by 4×. A real
monolithic run of the Oculus spec would have to hit one of three failure
modes around turn 75–100:

1. Hard context-window error (engine fails, session ends).
2. Auto-compaction with loss of earlier context (quality cost).
3. Self-imposed checkpoint/handoff patterns by the agent (staging-in-prompt).

The currently-running `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205` — a single-session rerun of the
same Oculus spec using the older `implement` engine — will provide the
empirical data point for which failure mode dominates.

## Caveats

1. **Pricing model is off by ~3× from actual.** My estimates inflate absolute
   dollar values by a factor of ~3 relative to what Claude billed (per the
   session YAML `cost_usd` fields). Possible causes: blended tier assumptions,
   Claude Code applying additional caching discounts I haven't modeled, or
   model-class differences. Ratios between scenarios are preserved.

2. **The monolithic simulation is idealized.** It assumes each turn's per-turn
   `input` and `output` work would be identical in a single session — in
   reality, a monolithic agent might take fewer turns (less orientation
   overhead) or more turns (confused by accumulated context). The projection
   is best read as a "what if the work pattern held constant" estimate, not
   a prediction.

3. **Claude's cache retrieval isn't perfect.** Real monolithic runs may show
   slightly lower cache_read than the simulation projects because of
   pruning, TTL expiry, or optimization. The simulation is a reasonable
   upper bound on context drag.

4. **No quality comparison.** This analysis is pure cost-and-volume. H2 (whether
   decomposed runs produce *better* output than monolithic runs) is unaddressed
   here and remains a separate parked inquiry.

5. **Two rigs is a small sample.** The `crd_slope` consistency across 11
   sessions gives some confidence it generalizes, but a richer corpus (the
   2026-04-03 analysis has 71 sessions) should be cross-checked. Notably the
   earlier artifact concluded "economic case for staged sessions is weak at
   current session lengths" — this newer analysis is more bullish on staging,
   and the difference is worth reconciling. Likely reasons: the 2026-04-03
   analysis didn't project monolithic costs for very long sessions (the
   dataset cut off well below the 267-turn Rig 2 scale), and didn't account
   for the hard context-window ceiling that dominates at that scale.

## Next Steps

- **Conclude `c-mo1w0d9a-16ddf9904d2c`** (this click) with the H1-weak-confirmed
  finding.
- **Wait for `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205`** to complete — its outcome is the
  H1-strong data point.
- **Reconcile with 2026-04-03 analysis** at some point, since the two arrive
  at different conclusions.
- **Design staged-sessions threshold** (parked H4 click `c-mo1w0rfk-2e0eeb3a1460`)
  — the crd_slope gives a first-order estimate: staging pays off above roughly
  `(200K context limit) / (crd_slope ≈ 2K per turn) ≈ 100 turns per session`.
  Rig 2's pieces averaged 44 turns — well below. A monolithic Rig 2 at 267
  turns is well above.

1	# Session Summary — 2026-04-16T21:01
2	
3	## What we did
4	
5	- Drilled into the planner-redesign umbrella (`c-mo1mq93f`); produced full dossier pulling the umbrella writ body, concluded/dropped clicks, transcripts, and scratch artifacts.
6	- Sean challenged the 4-tier decision model and the preference-model as not-MVP. I did the analytical work: enumerated candidate razor criteria, stress-tested against recent commissions, and proposed collapsing to a binary pick+record / surface split governed by a sharp razor.
7	- Mined all 38 `specs/*/decisions.yaml` files for patron overrides. Result: 35 overrides / ~945 decisions (**~3.7% baseline override rate**). 24 of 38 specs (63%) sailed through with zero overrides. Clustered overrides into six patterns; three of the six are codifiable policy preferences, not per-decision taste.
8	- Turned the analysis into 5 click changes under `c-mo1mq93f`: concluded the tier-model click; dropped the preference-model click; created+concluded three new clicks capturing the razor (`c-mo1yb8aj`), the three codified defaults (`c-mo1yb8nf`), and the empirical baseline (`c-mo1yb8zb`); cross-linked the razor to the atlas-staleness cluster.
9	- Dropped the handoff-notes trio (`c-mo1mqa1y` + children) as obsolete after X010 H1 findings.
10	- Concluded the umbrella click `c-mo1mq93f` with a six-pillar summary.
11	- Created draft brief writ **`w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63`** to commission the sage-instruction updates (scope expanded after Sean's prompt to cover all four sage roles — primary target is sage-reading-analyst where decisions actually surface).
12	- Added live click `c-mo1yf2y1` under `c-mo1itggx` noting that click-goal editability has surfaced as a need (immutability was explicit — not overriding, just logging).
13	
14	## Decisions made and/or deferred
15	
16	- **Scrap the 4-tier decision model.** Binary pick/surface + a razor is the MVP.
17	- **Drop the preference-model / correction-graduation automation.** Manual loop (patron reads records → updates instructions) suffices; ~40% of overrides are codifiable policy, not learnable taste.
18	- **Drop implement-loop handoff notes entirely.** X010 H1 reverted the default engine to single-session; decomposition's home is the parked lazy-decomp branch `c-mo1w08cs`.
19	- **Do NOT edit click umbrella goal text** (`c-mo1mq93f` still says "tiered decision model"). CLI has no edit command and Sean's immutability-by-design decision stands.
20	- **Conclude the umbrella even though operational follow-up remains.** The design is closed; implementation lives in the draft brief writ.
21	
22	## Next steps & open questions
23	
24	- Review and publish `w-mo1yp84y` when ready: `nsg writ show w-mo1yp84y && nsg writ publish w-mo1yp84y`.
25	- Re-measure override rate after sage updates ship; target below 2.5%.
26	- Open: whether click-goal editability is worth building; exhibit A is the stale `c-mo1mq93f` goal text.
27	
28	### Notable moments
29	
30	- **Sean reframing the whole tier question:** *"I'm not sure if the tiered model is the correct breakdown. I also feel like the 'self learning from past direction' is an aspirational goal, not an MVP one."* Cut both the tier structure and the self-learning ambition in one move. Notable because it shifted the design from a clever-mechanism model to a sharp-razor-plus-dumb-defaults model — exactly the kind of MVP discipline the autonomous-hopper goal depends on.
31	- **Sean redirecting scope at writ-creation time:** *"i guess its not really the writer only that needs to change, but also the reader-analyzer since that is where decisions surface."* Notable because it corrected my mental model of the Astrolabe pipeline — decisions surface during identification, not rendering. The razor needs to live upstream, not downstream.
32	- **Empirical pattern that stuck:** ~40% of patron overrides weren't value-judgments — they were Sean's already-expressed architectural taste (remove-over-deprecate, fail-loud, extend-API-not-route-around-it). Codifying taste is lower-cost than learning it.
33	
34	### Experiment observations
35	
36	- **X010 result used operationally.** The H1 falsification directly killed a sibling design line (handoff notes). Good example of experiment findings feeding live design decisions rather than sitting in artifacts.
37	- **Baseline measurement `c-mo1yb8zb` is a standing instrument.** Future comparisons depend on it being re-runnable — the mining was a simple yq extraction over `specs/*/decisions.yaml`, worth preserving as a script.
38	
39	## Reminder for Sean
40	
41	**Ethnographer interview is overdue.** Most recent was 2026-04-07 (9 days ago). Might be a good time to check in with the ethnographer.

# Session Summary — 2026-04-16T21:01

## What we did

- Drilled into the planner-redesign umbrella (`c-mo1mq93f`); produced full dossier pulling the umbrella writ body, concluded/dropped clicks, transcripts, and scratch artifacts.
- Sean challenged the 4-tier decision model and the preference-model as not-MVP. I did the analytical work: enumerated candidate razor criteria, stress-tested against recent commissions, and proposed collapsing to a binary pick+record / surface split governed by a sharp razor.
- Mined all 38 `specs/*/decisions.yaml` files for patron overrides. Result: 35 overrides / ~945 decisions (**~3.7% baseline override rate**). 24 of 38 specs (63%) sailed through with zero overrides. Clustered overrides into six patterns; three of the six are codifiable policy preferences, not per-decision taste.
- Turned the analysis into 5 click changes under `c-mo1mq93f`: concluded the tier-model click; dropped the preference-model click; created+concluded three new clicks capturing the razor (`c-mo1yb8aj`), the three codified defaults (`c-mo1yb8nf`), and the empirical baseline (`c-mo1yb8zb`); cross-linked the razor to the atlas-staleness cluster.
- Dropped the handoff-notes trio (`c-mo1mqa1y` + children) as obsolete after X010 H1 findings.
- Concluded the umbrella click `c-mo1mq93f` with a six-pillar summary.
- Created draft brief writ **`w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63`** to commission the sage-instruction updates (scope expanded after Sean's prompt to cover all four sage roles — primary target is sage-reading-analyst where decisions actually surface).
- Added live click `c-mo1yf2y1` under `c-mo1itggx` noting that click-goal editability has surfaced as a need (immutability was explicit — not overriding, just logging).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Scrap the 4-tier decision model.** Binary pick/surface + a razor is the MVP.
- **Drop the preference-model / correction-graduation automation.** Manual loop (patron reads records → updates instructions) suffices; ~40% of overrides are codifiable policy, not learnable taste.
- **Drop implement-loop handoff notes entirely.** X010 H1 reverted the default engine to single-session; decomposition's home is the parked lazy-decomp branch `c-mo1w08cs`.
- **Do NOT edit click umbrella goal text** (`c-mo1mq93f` still says "tiered decision model"). CLI has no edit command and Sean's immutability-by-design decision stands.
- **Conclude the umbrella even though operational follow-up remains.** The design is closed; implementation lives in the draft brief writ.

## Next steps & open questions

- Review and publish `w-mo1yp84y` when ready: `nsg writ show w-mo1yp84y && nsg writ publish w-mo1yp84y`.
- Re-measure override rate after sage updates ship; target below 2.5%.
- Open: whether click-goal editability is worth building; exhibit A is the stale `c-mo1mq93f` goal text.

### Notable moments

- **Sean reframing the whole tier question:** *"I'm not sure if the tiered model is the correct breakdown. I also feel like the 'self learning from past direction' is an aspirational goal, not an MVP one."* Cut both the tier structure and the self-learning ambition in one move. Notable because it shifted the design from a clever-mechanism model to a sharp-razor-plus-dumb-defaults model — exactly the kind of MVP discipline the autonomous-hopper goal depends on.
- **Sean redirecting scope at writ-creation time:** *"i guess its not really the writer only that needs to change, but also the reader-analyzer since that is where decisions surface."* Notable because it corrected my mental model of the Astrolabe pipeline — decisions surface during identification, not rendering. The razor needs to live upstream, not downstream.
- **Empirical pattern that stuck:** ~40% of patron overrides weren't value-judgments — they were Sean's already-expressed architectural taste (remove-over-deprecate, fail-loud, extend-API-not-route-around-it). Codifying taste is lower-cost than learning it.

### Experiment observations

- **X010 result used operationally.** The H1 falsification directly killed a sibling design line (handoff notes). Good example of experiment findings feeding live design decisions rather than sitting in artifacts.
- **Baseline measurement `c-mo1yb8zb` is a standing instrument.** Future comparisons depend on it being re-runnable — the mining was a simple yq extraction over `specs/*/decisions.yaml`, worth preserving as a script.

## Reminder for Sean

**Ethnographer interview is overdue.** Most recent was 2026-04-07 (9 days ago). Might be a good time to check in with the ethnographer.

content.content:
1	# X010 H1 — Monolithic Baseline: Cheap Mining Projection Falsified
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-04-16
4	**Hypothesis tested:** H1 — Long sessions accumulate marginal cost that staging avoids.
5	**Method:** Run the same Oculus click-page spec through a single-session `implement`
6	engine (same writ body as the decomposed Rig 2); compare observed cost, turn count,
7	wall time, and quality to the decomposed run.
8	**Rig:** `rig-mo1wajm9-8bf5d205` (implement engine, mandate `w-mo1wagye-441cbcee1849`)
9	**Click:** `c-mo1w0g2n-4b206fe845e5` (H1 monolithic baseline, concluded by this artifact)
10	**Supersedes the projection in:** `2026-04-16-h1-cheap-mining.md`
11	
12	## TL;DR
13	
14	**H1 is falsified at the scale we tested.** The monolithic run beat the decomposed
15	run on every axis: cost, wall time, turn count, token usage, seal reliability,
16	and review-pass cleanliness. The cheap-mining projection (which predicted
17	monolithic would be 3.4× more expensive) was wrong by a factor of **~9×** in
18	the opposite direction — actual monolithic ran at **0.38× decomposed cost**.
19	
20	| Metric | Monolithic | Decomposed Rig 2 | mono/decomp |
21	|---|---:|---:|---:|
22	| Turns (implement only) | 77 | 267 | **0.29×** |
23	| Wall time (implement) | 749 s (12.5 min) | 2,717 s (45 min) | **0.28×** |
24	| input tokens | 97 | 327 | 0.30× |
25	| cache_create tokens | 274,560 | 815,450 | 0.34× |
26	| cache_read tokens | 7,559,290 | 20,587,945 | 0.37× |
27	| output tokens | 51,730 | 132,504 | 0.39× |
28	| **billed cost (impl)** | **$7.07** | **$18.58** | **0.38×** |
29	| Review outcome | PASS, no required changes | PASS, no required changes | tie |
30	| Seal outcome | **fast-forward**, 0 retries | **FAILED**, rebase conflict | mono wins |
31	| Total rig cost (impl+review+revise) | $9.18 | $20.11 | 0.46× |
32	
33	## What Actually Happened
34	
35	The setup Sean proposed (swap `implement-loop` → `implement` in `guild.json`,
36	repost the Oculus mandate body verbatim) worked cleanly. The older `implement`
37	engine summoned a single Claude session with the full 7k-line spec body plus
38	`EXECUTION_EPILOGUE`. 12.5 minutes later, the agent had produced 2 commits
39	covering all 6 tasks, the review engine passed with only cosmetic notes, and
40	the seal fast-forwarded onto main.
41	
42	**The run did hit an auto-compaction event.** Inspection of the transcript
43	(`/home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspace-vibers-tmp-nexus-drafts-nexus-draft-mo1wak9c-9415c244/99c46df8-e422-4efa-b8d5-aec28d9f7089.jsonl`)
44	shows:
45	
46	- Turns 1–62: cache_read grew from 14,440 to 166,431 tokens (within the
47	  ~200K Opus window).
48	- **Turn 63: cache_read drops to 14,533.** This is Claude Code's
49	  auto-compaction — the agent's context was summarized and reset.
50	- Turns 63–77: cache_read rebuilt to 77,090 by session end.
51	
52	The compaction was seamless — no error, no quality drop observable in the
53	outcome (review passed cleanly). My projection predicted a hard ceiling at
54	turn ~100 causing failure or truncation; actual behavior is that
55	**auto-compaction is a graceful soft ceiling.**
56	
57	> **Correction (post-H2 audit, 2026-04-16):** a second-pass audit found that
58	> the session actually had *two* compaction events, not one. An earlier
59	> compaction at **turn 11** (cache_read 56,900 → 14,533) preceded the
60	> turn-63 event. It fires during the early exploration phase — before any
61	> file edits had happened — so it doesn't affect any of this artifact's
62	> conclusions, but the "one compaction" framing here is inaccurate. See
63	> click `c-mo1y846y-5ce86d5155f2` for the full three-phase breakdown
64	> (Phase A: turns 1–10 exploration; Phase B: 11–62 main work; Phase C:
65	> 63–77 test authoring) and the post-compaction-2 quality audit.
66	
67	## Why The Cheap-Mining Projection Was Wrong
68	
69	The projection's fatal assumption: *same turn count, compounding cache-read.*
70	
71	That held neither part.
72	
73	### 1. Turn count was 29% of what I projected
74	
75	My projection used the decomposed rig's 267 turns and asked "what if all that
76	were in one session?" In reality, the monolithic agent did the same work in
77	**77 turns — 0.29× the decomposed total.**
78	
79	Where did the extra 190 decomposed turns go? Mostly re-orientation:
80	
81	- Each piece-session starts cold — reads the spec, greps the codebase, runs
82	  `git log` / `git status`, verifies the branch, reads adjacent files.
83	- Each piece re-explains what it's about to do ("I'll now implement t3 as
84	  specified...") before making edits.
85	- Each piece commits separately, often with multi-turn commit-message
86	  drafting.
87	- The agent often spends 3–5 turns at the start of a piece validating that
88	  prior pieces landed correctly.
89	
90	**The monolithic agent paid this orientation cost exactly once.** It then
91	ran through the 6 tasks as a continuous flow, reusing its warm mental model
92	of the codebase and spec. This is the opposite of what the naive "staging
93	reduces context drag" intuition suggests.
94	
95	### 2. Cache-read per turn was higher monolithic, but turn count dominated
96	
97	- Monolithic mean cache_read/turn: 98,173 tokens
98	- Decomposed (Rig 2) mean cache_read/turn: 77,108 tokens
99	
100	Per-turn, the monolithic run carried ~27% more context drag (as expected — longer
101	continuous session means more context to replay). But with 0.29× the turns, the
102	aggregate cache-read was 0.37× decomposed. **Turn count is the dominant lever;
103	per-turn cost is secondary.**
104	
105	### 3. Output tokens showed the biggest savings
106	
107	- Monolithic total output: 51,730
108	- Decomposed Rig 2 total output: 132,504
109	- **Ratio: 0.39×**
110	
111	Output is the most expensive token lane ($75/M vs $1.50/M cache-read). The
112	40% ratio here drove most of the dollar savings. Decomposed sessions generate
113	a lot of explanation, status-updates, and plan-restating text per piece —
114	none of which contributes to the artifact.
115	
116	### 4. Auto-compaction saves you from the context ceiling
117	
118	The compaction event at turn 63 proves Claude Code handles long-session
119	context gracefully. I feared a hard failure mode; the real failure mode is
120	"Claude summarizes your context and continues." On this run, the
121	post-compaction session (turns 63–77) completed the remaining work and
122	passed review cleanly. No observable quality cost.
123	
124	For this size of spec, **the context ceiling is effectively not a wall but
125	a speed bump.**
126	
127	## What Decomposition Actually Buys (on this dataset)
128	
129	Given the dramatic cost, time, and reliability upset, what's the remaining
130	case for decomposition?
131	
132	- **None observed on this single-spec comparison.** Decomposed cost 2.6×
133	  more money, took 3.6× longer wall, had a seal failure the monolithic run
134	  avoided, and produced functionally equivalent output (both PASS review).
135	- **Commits were granular in decomposed (6 commits vs 2 in monolithic).**
136	  That's the one axis where decomposition wins — finer-grained git history.
137	  Whether it's worth a 2.6× cost premium is the real question.
138	
139	It's possible decomposition shines at specs larger than this one — but this
140	spec was already 25 decisions and ~7k lines of body, which I'd have called
141	"large" before running it.
142	
143	## Implications for H-series Hypotheses
144	
145	- **H1 (long sessions accumulate marginal cost):** Falsified at this scale.
146	  The opposite holds: decomposed sessions accumulate more total cost
147	  because orientation tax is paid per piece. Filing as refuted pending a
148	  larger-spec test.
149	- **H2 (shorter sessions produce better quality):** Not supported by this
150	  data — both runs passed review cleanly. Worth running the parked H2
151	  clicks anyway since N=1 is weak evidence.
152	- **H3 (systemic costs favor shorter sessions):** Partially refuted. The
153	  monolithic run had a clean seal with zero rebase conflicts; the decomposed
154	  run had a seal-failing rebase conflict. Shorter wall time (12.5 min vs
155	  45 min) meant less overlap with other activity on main. This is the
156	  opposite of what H3's framing expected.
157	- **H4 (above what threshold does staging pay off):** This test finds the
158	  **threshold is above the Oculus-spec size, not below it.** We don't
159	  have a measured threshold above which staging helps — which itself
160	  argues that the threshold is either nonexistent for realistic specs or
161	  lives at a scale we haven't sampled.
162	- **Lazy decomposition:** Still potentially interesting since it sidesteps
163	  the orientation-per-piece tax (agent self-stops when coherent, rather
164	  than a planner pre-slicing). But the urgency of that inquiry drops
165	  given monolithic already works well.
166	
167	## Caveats
168	
169	1. **N=1 for the spec.** One monolithic run vs one decomposed run. Both
170	   passed review, both used the same spec body, same model, same
171	   workspace. But a single spec is a single data point — the effect could
172	   vary dramatically with spec shape (refactoring vs greenfield, heavy
173	   exploration vs heavy writing, etc).
174	
175	2. **The spec was pre-planned.** Astrolabe had already planned the Oculus
176	   spec into 6 tasks with a manifest. The monolithic agent received those
177	   tasks as structured guidance in-prompt, so it still benefited from
178	   planning-time decomposition (just not execution-time decomposition).
179	   This is a variable we haven't isolated.
180	
181	3. **Auto-compaction is model-version-dependent.** Claude Code 2.1.111
182	   (the version running these sessions) has mature compaction. Older or
183	   differently-configured agents may not compact as gracefully. If we
184	   change models or providers, this finding may not transfer.
185	
186	4. **Quality assessment is shallow.** Both runs passed review — but review
187	   is a cheap check. A deeper quality audit (integration correctness,
188	   long-term maintainability, edge-case handling) might differentiate.
189

193	   run that happened to finish in 12.5 min would likely seal cleanly too.
